unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9, the final unit of Year 8. This unit is called "Music as Universal Language" because you will explore how music, performance, and the creative arts bring people together across cultures, languages, and backgrounds. Music is one of the most powerful forms of human expression. It can communicate emotions that words alone cannot capture. A song can make you feel joy, sorrow, excitement, or peace without you understanding a single word of the language in which it is sung. That is why we call music a universal language: it speaks to everyone.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

readings.title: Music as Universal Language: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
🧭 How to Use This Unit
This is your main lesson file. Work through it section by section at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar, and the Story "The Curtain Never Lies".
Start with the Vocabulary file to learn the key words, then come back here for Reading, Grammar, Writing, and Speaking.
Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck or want extra practice. Bring your finished work to your teacher sessions.
Welcome to Unit 9, the final unit of Year 8. This unit is called "Music as Universal Language" because you will explore how music, performance, and the creative arts bring people together across cultures, languages, and backgrounds. Music is one of the most powerful forms of human expression. It can communicate emotions that words alone cannot capture. A song can make you feel joy, sorrow, excitement, or peace without you understanding a single word of the language in which it is sung. That is why we call music a universal language: it speaks to everyone.
In this unit, you will read a story called "The Curtain Never Lies" about a group of young East African students who face a dramatic challenge during a regional performing arts competition. Through their experience, you will explore themes of creativity, integrity, resilience, and the power of honest artistic expression. You will also study the Present Perfect Continuous tense, which allows you to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening or have only recently stopped. You will learn about participle clauses for more sophisticated sentence structures, what clauses for expressing strong feelings, and polite question structures for respectful communication.
Throughout this unit, you will develop your reading comprehension, writing, speaking, and listening skills through a range of activities connected to the theme of music and performance. By the end of this unit, you will be able to write confidently about ongoing actions and recent experiences, express strong emotions using exclamatory sentences, and communicate politely and clearly in formal and informal situations. These are skills that will serve you well in Year 9 and beyond.
In Islam, the arts and creativity hold an important place. Allah is Al-Musawwir, the Fashioner, and He has given human beings the ability to create, to express, and to communicate through beautiful forms. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said that Allah is beautiful and loves beauty. When we use our creative talents honestly and with good intention, we honour the gifts that Allah has given us. This unit will help you think about what it means to be an honest artist, a supportive teammate, and a person of integrity, whether you are on a stage or in everyday life.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what it means to call music a universal language and discuss how the performing arts connect people across cultures.
Read the story "The Curtain Never Lies" and answer comprehension and analysis questions using evidence from the text.
Use thirty key vocabulary words related to performance, creativity, emotion, and integrity in your own speaking and writing.
Form and use the Present Perfect Continuous tense correctly to describe actions that started in the past and are still continuing or have recently stopped.
Identify and create participle clauses to combine sentences and add detail in a more sophisticated way.
Use what clauses to form exclamatory sentences that express strong feelings or reactions.
Ask polite questions using appropriate structures such as "Could you...?", "Would you mind...?", and "Do you know...?"
Use reported speech to convey what someone else said without quoting their exact words.
Use reflexive pronouns correctly when the subject and object of a sentence are the same person.
Write a performance review of a play, concert, or artistic event using appropriate vocabulary and grammar.
Write a persuasive speech about the importance of the arts in education or community life.

readings.title: Music as Universal Language: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Take part in a role-play interview with a performer, a group discussion about music and culture, and a dramatic reading of a scene.
Reflect on your own learning using self-assessment statements and set goals for further improvement.
The words below are the most important new vocabulary for this unit. You will meet them throughout the story, the grammar exercises, and the writing activities. Read each definition carefully and study the example sentence. Try to use at least five of these words in your own writing this week. Your full vocabulary companion file contains all thirty unit words with extended practice activities, so make sure you study that file as well.
The word 'music' comes from the ancient Greek word 'mousike', which originally
meant 'art of the Muses'. The Muses were goddesses of inspiration in Greek
mythology. In many cultures around the world, music and poetry are considered
divine gifts. The Arabic word for music, 'musiqa', shares this same root.
The main grammar focus of this unit is the Present Perfect Continuous tense. This tense is used to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or that were happening recently and have just stopped. It emphasises the duration or ongoing nature of an activity, and it is one of the most useful tenses for describing experiences and recent events.
Structure: Subject + has/have + been + verb(-ing)
Use has been with he, she, it: "She has been practising the piano for two hours."
Use have been with I, you, we, they: "They have been rehearsing since morning."
Use the Present Perfect Continuous when you want to show:
An action that started in the past and is still continuing: "I have been studying English for three years." (You started three years ago and you are still studying.)
An action that recently stopped but has a visible result: "She has been crying — her eyes are red." (She stopped crying, but you can see the result.)
Duration of an ongoing activity: "They have been waiting for the bus for twenty minutes." (The waiting started twenty minutes ago and continues now.)
The Present Perfect Continuous is often used with time words that show duration:
for + period of time: "He has been performing for five years."
since + starting point: "We have been rehearsing since Monday."
all day / all morning / all week: "She has been composing music all day."
how long: "How long have you been learning to play the drums?"
Mistake 1: Forgetting to include "been".
Incorrect: "She has practising the guitar."
Correct: "She has been practising the guitar."
Mistake 2: Using the wrong form of "have".
Incorrect: "He have been singing all morning."
Correct: "He has been singing all morning."
Mistake 3: Using the base form instead of the -ing form.
Incorrect: "They have been practice every day."
Correct: "They have been practising every day."
Your full Grammar companion file contains detailed lessons on the Present Perfect Continuous and all the other grammar points for this unit, with many more examples and practice exercises. Make sure you study that file carefully.

Reading and Listening: The Curtain Never Lies
The story for this unit is called "The Curtain Never Lies." It follows a group of students from Musa Academy in Kampala, Uganda, as they compete in a regional performing arts competition. The story explores themes of creativity, teamwork, artistic integrity, and the universal power of performance. You will find the full story in your Story companion file. Read it carefully, and then return here to answer the comprehension questions below.
As you read, pay attention to the following:
The vocabulary words from this unit that appear in the story. Say each one out loud when you find it.
The grammar structures used: Present Perfect Continuous, participle clauses, what clauses, and polite questions.

readings.title: 📖 About This Story, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Genre: Realistic fiction with elements of suspense and personal growth.
Theme: Creativity, integrity, resilience, and the universal power of performance.
Setting: A modern performing arts centre in Kampala, Uganda.
Main characters: Isaac and Samira, students at Musa Academy.
Moral: Art only matters when it is honest. Applause means little if you have sullied your voice to earn it.
Read slowly and carefully. When you meet a word from your Vocabulary list, say it out loud!
Kampala's Youth Performing Arts Centre pulsed with anticipation. Rows of seats were filling under chandeliers shaped like butterflies. The regional finals were tonight, and schools from across East Africa had sent their best.
In the velvet-draped dressing room, the students from Musa Academy moved with practised energy. Among them was Isaac, tall and focused, adjusting a homemade collar for his lead costume. His fingers were deft, his face calm. He had been mustering the courage to direct his own play for over a year, and now the moment had finally materialized.
Beside him, Samira painted on freckles. "Do you think the crowd will get our theme?" she asked.
"Only if we render it clearly," Isaac replied. He paused. "Do you know what 'render' really means in theatre terms?"
She grinned. "To perform, to evoke, to cause something to be real. I have been paying attention."
Their piece, The Last Contortion, followed a young dancer who faked injuries to manipulate her team. It was meant as a metaphor for artistic honesty.
"What a formidable concept," their literature teacher, Ustadha Nadia, had said when they first proposed it. "But risky."
Backstage, Isaac noticed another team — a sleek group from a private school in Nairobi. Their props gleamed. Their costumes shimmered.
"That is Horizon East," someone whispered. "They are notorious for dramatic openings. And drama off-stage too."
Indeed, Horizon East had a reputation. Last year, they had been accused of sabotaging another team's lighting script.
Isaac kept his distance. But later, when he returned to the wings after a break, he found one of their costume bags half-open near his own.
Suspicious, he quickly checked his team's prop trunk.
The final scene's concoction — a symbolic mask, torn between comedy and tragedy — was missing.
"What happened?" Samira asked, seeing his frozen expression.
Isaac's voice dropped. "The mask is gone."
Part 2: The Show Must Go On
He went straight to the organiser's desk. "Someone has taken one of our props. It is central to our climax."
A tall woman with a clipboard looked up. "We cannot delay the show, but I will ask around."
Samira pulled him aside. "Do not let this cause havoc in your head. We can still perform."
Isaac stared ahead. "Without the mask, the ending loses weight."
"Then improvise. Your story matters more than a mask."
He inhaled deeply. "You are right. Let us get ready."
They stepped onstage. Lights hit. Music began.
Rendered spotlight-blind, Isaac moved through the blocking. Each line hit its mark. The audience leaned in.
Where the mask should have been, Samira held up a cloth scrap.
Isaac hesitated — then stepped forward and tore the cloth into two halves, holding one to each side of his face.
"Comedy or tragedy," he cried, "we make the mask we wear!"
The crowd gasped. Some stood. Applause thundered.
Part 3: After the Curtain Falls
Backstage, Horizon East's leader passed by.
"Did you take it?" Isaac asked.
She shrugged. "Could you prove it?"
He glared. "No. But your behaviour was repugnant."
Later, Samira approached him. "You handled that better than I ever could."
He smiled, exhausted. "Honestly, I wanted to charge headlong into their dressing room."
"But you did not. You performed."
"Do you think we will win?"
"I do not know," she said. "But what a performance!"
Back in the holding room, they sat in quiet tension. One boy meandered in loops near the window. Another girl rubbed her temples.

readings.title: 📖 About This Story, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The announcer returned. "Third place: Horizon East."
"And the winner of this year's regional youth drama finals — Musa Academy!"
Cheers erupted. Isaac sat stunned. Samira hugged him.
"What an impulsive moment you had," she teased. "And it saved the show."
Weeks later, a judge's email materialized in Isaac's inbox:
"You reminded us all why theatre matters — because even when stripped of perfection, it reveals truth."
Isaac smiled. That night, he wrote in his journal:
"I nearly gave in to fear. I almost let them sully the work we had built. But we stood tall. Rendered vulnerable, we became stronger."
After the competition, Musa Academy was invited to perform the piece again — this time at the Kampala National Theatre. It was a chance to rebuild the show with all the original props and polish every moment.
They had only ten days to prepare, and the process was gruelling. Lights had to be adjusted. The musical cues had to be timed perfectly. Even the transitions between scenes were rehearsed until their feet ached.
Isaac noticed how fatigue tested everyone. Some team members grew aloof. Tempers rose. There were disagreements about lines, costumes, and entrances. One night, a heated argument broke out over whether a side character should speak or mime.
"I just want it to be accurate," one actor insisted. "We need to preserve what worked."
"But sometimes art evolves," Isaac said, trying to mediate. "We can adjust. The moment will tell us."
Despite the pressure, the team bonded in new ways. They took turns fending off stress with dance breaks and storytelling circles. They joked about their first performance, about how Isaac had almost cried backstage.
On the eve of the show, Ustadha Nadia visited during their final rehearsal. "You have grown," she said after the run. "Not just as a performer. As a leader."
Opening night arrived. The theatre was full. As the curtain rose, Isaac stepped onstage with his ensemble. This time, every prop was in place. But more importantly, their integrity was intact.
The performance flowed with grace and fire. When the mask finally appeared in the final scene, the crowd gasped as though seeing a ghost return.
"What a story," someone whispered aloud. "What a choice."
And when the play ended, the applause was long, loud, and real.
After their second performance, the cast was approached by two international festival coordinators. One was from Johannesburg's Youth Vision Festival, and the other represented the Nairobi Dramatic Society.
"Would it be too bold," the man from Johannesburg asked politely, "if we invited you to present your play at our July showcase?"
The students were stunned. Isaac blinked, unsure what to say. He glanced at Ustadha Nadia, who nodded gently.
"Yes," Isaac replied. "It would be an honour."
The following weeks were filled with planning and preparation. New scripts were printed. Costumes were adjusted. They practised not only the performance but how to speak in interviews, how to answer questions from international press, and how to carry themselves in front of strangers.
Isaac found himself reflecting more deeply on what their play had meant — not just to the audience, but to himself. He realised how it mirrored the journey they had all taken: learning to confront insecurity, to resist dishonesty, and to find courage in vulnerability.
At the Johannesburg showcase, Musa Academy performed before a thousand people. The applause that night was thunderous, but what meant more to Isaac was a conversation with a young boy who approached him after the show.
"That play made me cry," the boy said. "Because I lie sometimes too. But I do not want to anymore."
That night, sitting alone on the edge of the performance tent, Isaac scribbled in his notebook:
"This is what theatre does. It reflects us — so we can see what to change. And sometimes, it reflects what we already are. Just hidden."

readings.title: 📖 About This Story, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Back home in Kampala, their return was quiet. No parades. Just school as usual. But something had shifted. Teachers gave them respectful nods. Younger students began writing plays of their own.
One Friday, Ustadha Nadia held a lunchtime seminar on ethics in performance. Isaac was asked to co-lead it.
"What is the most important thing a young artist should remember?" she asked.
Isaac paused. Then he answered, "Do not just ask what you want to say. Ask what your story does to people."

grammar.title: Present Perfect Continuous
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The Present Perfect Continuous tense is used to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or that were happening recently and have just stopped. This tense emphasises the duration and ongoing nature of the action. It is one of the most useful tenses for describing recent experiences, long-running activities, and situations where you can see the visible result of an activity that has been in progress. For example, if your friend's hands are covered in paint, you might say, "You have been painting!" The painting may have stopped, but the evidence is still visible.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The Present Perfect Continuous tense is used to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or that were happening recently and have just stopped. This tense emphasises the duration and ongoing nature of the action. It is one of the most useful tenses for describing recent experiences, long-running activities, and situations where you can see the visible result of an activity that has been in progress. For example, if your friend's hands are covered in paint, you might say, "You have been painting!" The painting may have stopped, but the evidence is still visible.
How to form it
Structure: Subject + has/have + been + verb(-ing)
Use has been with he, she, it: "She has been mustering the courage to perform."
Use have been with I, you, we, they: "They have been rehearsing since morning."
Negative: Subject + has/have + not + been + verb(-ing)
"She has not been practising enough this week."
"They have not been paying attention during rehearsals."
Question: Has/Have + subject + been + verb(-ing)?
"Has she been mustering the courage to perform?" — "Yes, she has."
"Have they been rehearsing all day?" — "Yes, they have."
When to use it
Use the Present Perfect Continuous for:
Actions still continuing: "I have been studying English for three years." (You started three years ago and you are still studying now.)
Recent actions with visible results: "She has been crying — her eyes are red." (She stopped, but you can see the evidence.)
Duration of ongoing activities: "They have been waiting for twenty minutes." (The waiting started twenty minutes ago and continues.)
Repeated actions over a period: "He has been performing in school plays since Year 6." (He started in Year 6 and still performs now.)
Key time words
The Present Perfect Continuous is often used with:
for + period: "He has been performing for five years."
since + starting point: "We have been rehearsing since Monday."
all day / all morning / all week: "She has been composing music all day."
how long: "How long have you been learning the drums?"
lately / recently: "I have been feeling more confident lately."
Present Perfect Continuous vs Present Perfect Simple
These two tenses are sometimes confused. The key difference is that the Present Perfect Continuous emphasises the process or duration, while the Present Perfect Simple emphasises the result or completion.
"I have been reading this book all week." (Focus on the ongoing process — you are still reading it.)
"I have read three chapters." (Focus on the result — three chapters are finished.)
"She has been practising the piano for two hours." (Focus on the duration.)
"She has practised the piano." (Focus on the fact that the practice happened.)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Forgetting "been".
Incorrect: "She has mustering the courage."
Correct: "She has been mustering the courage."
Mistake 2: Wrong form of "have".
Incorrect: "They has been studying."
Correct: "They have been studying."
Mistake 3: Using the base verb instead of -ing form.
Incorrect: "She has been muster the courage."
Correct: "She has been mustering the courage."
Mistake 4: Using the Present Perfect Continuous with stative verbs that do not normally take -ing.
Incorrect: "I have been knowing him for years."
Correct: "I have known him for years." ("Know" is a stative verb — use Present Perfect Simple.)
Quick memory trick
Think of the Present Perfect Continuous as the "how long" tense. If you can ask "How long have you been doing this?" and the answer shows time spent on an ongoing activity, you are using this tense correctly.
Practice
Complete the sentences using the Present Perfect Continuous:
I __________ (play) the guitar for three hours.
He __________ (learn) to dance since last summer.
They __________ (wait) for the bus for twenty minutes.
She __________ (muster) the courage to perform all week.
We __________ (rehearse) this scene since Monday.
How long __________ you __________ (practise) the piano?
Answers: 1. have been playing  2. has been learning  3. have been waiting  4. has been mustering  5. have been rehearsing  6. have ... been practising

grammar.title: Participle Clauses
grammar.explanation:
What they are and why they matter
A participle clause is a special kind of phrase that helps you combine sentences in a smooth, elegant way. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can join them using a participle to add extra information about the subject. This makes your writing more concise and sophisticated. For example, instead of saying "She was rendered breathless by the performance. She collapsed backstage," you can say, "Rendered breathless, she collapsed backstage." The participle clause saves space and makes your sentence flow better.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they are and why they matter
A participle clause is a special kind of phrase that helps you combine sentences in a smooth, elegant way. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can join them using a participle to add extra information about the subject. This makes your writing more concise and sophisticated. For example, instead of saying "She was rendered breathless by the performance. She collapsed backstage," you can say, "Rendered breathless, she collapsed backstage." The participle clause saves space and makes your sentence flow better.
Types of participles
Present participles end in -ing:
running, swimming, feeling, performing, mustering
Past participles usually end in -ed (or have irregular forms):
excited, exhausted, rendered, finished, inspired, hidden, torn, written
How to form participle clauses
Identify the main clause: "She collapsed backstage."
Identify the extra information: "She was rendered breathless."
Turn the extra information into a participle clause: "Rendered breathless" (remove "She was").
Combine: "Rendered breathless, she collapsed backstage."
More examples from the story and the unit theme:
"Rendered spotlight-blind, Isaac moved through the blocking." (Past participle — describes Isaac's state.)
"Feeling tired, he went to bed early." (Present participle — describes the reason.)
"Excited about the showcase, she packed her costume with care." (Past participle — describes the emotion.)
"Having rehearsed all day, they were exhausted by evening." (Perfect participle — shows the earlier action.)
Important rule
The subject of the participle clause must be the same as the subject of the main clause. If they are different, the sentence will sound wrong or be confusing.
Incorrect: "Feeling hungry, the pizza was eaten." (This says the pizza felt hungry!)
Correct: "Feeling hungry, he ate the pizza." (He felt hungry, and he ate.)
Participle clauses for different meanings
Participle clauses can show:
Reason: "Worried about the exam, he studied late into the night." (Why did he study? Because he was worried.)
Time: "Arriving at the theatre, she noticed the long queue." (When did she notice? When she arrived.)
Result: "The power went out, plunging the stage into darkness." (What was the result? Darkness.)
Manner: "He left the stage, bowing to the audience." (How did he leave? Bowing.)
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Mismatched subjects (dangling participle).
Incorrect: "Walking to the theatre, the rain started." (The rain was not walking!)
Correct: "Walking to the theatre, she got caught in the rain."
Mistake 2: Using the wrong participle form.
Incorrect: "She was exciting to see the performance."
Correct: "She was excited to see the performance."
Practice
Combine each pair of sentences using a participle clause:
She was excited by the news. She jumped up and down.
He was tired from running. He sat down on the grass.
She was surprised by the gift. She could not stop smiling.
He was inspired by the story. He began writing his own play.
They were determined to succeed. They rehearsed every evening.
Answers: 1. Excited by the news, she jumped up and down. 2. Tired from running, he sat down on the grass. 3. Surprised by the gift, she could not stop smiling. 4. Inspired by the story, he began writing his own play. 5. Determined to succeed, they rehearsed every evening.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply participle clauses in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: What Clauses for Exclamations
grammar.explanation:
What they are and why they matter
Exclamatory sentences using "what" are a lively way to express strong feelings or reactions such as excitement, surprise, or admiration. In the story, characters use what clauses at moments of high emotion: "What a formidable concept!" and "What a performance!" Learning to use these structures will make your spoken and written English more expressive and engaging.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they are and why they matter
Exclamatory sentences using "what" are a lively way to express strong feelings or reactions such as excitement, surprise, or admiration. In the story, characters use what clauses at moments of high emotion: "What a formidable concept!" and "What a performance!" Learning to use these structures will make your spoken and written English more expressive and engaging.
How to form what clauses
Structure: What + a/an + adjective + noun + !
Examples:
"What a formidable performance!"
"What an impulsive act!"
"What a wonderful surprise!"
"What an incredible adventure!"
With plural or uncountable nouns (no "a/an"):
"What talented musicians!" (plural)
"What beautiful music!" (uncountable)
"What deft fingers!" (plural)
What vs How
Both "what" and "how" can begin exclamations, but they follow different patterns:
What + adjective + noun: "What a beautiful song!"
How + adjective (no noun): "How beautiful!"
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using "How" with a noun instead of "What".
Incorrect: "How a beautiful day!"
Correct: "What a beautiful day!"
Mistake 2: Forgetting the adjective.
Incorrect: "What dog!"
Correct: "What a cute dog!"
Mistake 3: Using "a" before a plural or uncountable noun.
Incorrect: "What a talented musicians!"
Correct: "What talented musicians!"
Quick memory trick
Think of "What" as a magic word that opens the door to your feelings. Whenever you see or hear something amazing, just remember: "What a..." followed by an adjective and a noun!
Practice
Complete each exclamation using an appropriate adjective and noun:
What a __________ __________! (about a very impressive concert)
What an __________ __________! (about a surprising ending to a play)
What __________ __________! (about a group of very skilled dancers)
What a __________ __________! (about a mask that was very elaborate)
What __________ __________! (about music that is incredibly moving)
Suggested Answers: 1. What a spectacular concert! 2. What an unexpected ending! 3. What skilled dancers! 4. What an elaborate mask! 5. What moving music!
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what clauses for exclamations in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Polite Question Structures
grammar.explanation:
What they are and why they matter
Polite question structures help you communicate respectfully and considerately. When you ask questions politely, you show that you value the other person's time and feelings. This is especially important in formal situations, when talking to adults, or when meeting people for the first time. In the story, characters use polite questions in several places, such as "Do you know what 'render' really means in theatre terms?" and "Would it be too bold if we invited you to present your play?"
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they are and why they matter
Polite question structures help you communicate respectfully and considerately. When you ask questions politely, you show that you value the other person's time and feelings. This is especially important in formal situations, when talking to adults, or when meeting people for the first time. In the story, characters use polite questions in several places, such as "Do you know what 'render' really means in theatre terms?" and "Would it be too bold if we invited you to present your play?"
Common polite question structures
1. Could you...? / Can you...?
"Could you tell me where the rehearsal room is?"
"Could you please explain that scene again?"
2. Would you mind...? (+ verb-ing)
"Would you mind passing the script?"
"Would you mind repeating the instructions?"
3. Do you know...? (embedded question — statement word order)
"Do you know what 'meander' means?" (Not: "Do you know what does 'meander' mean?")
"Do you know where the theatre is?" (Not: "Do you know where is the theatre?")
4. Would you be able to...?
"Would you be able to help me with the costumes?"
"Would you be able to attend the rehearsal tomorrow?"
5. I was wondering if...? (very polite / formal)
"I was wondering if you could give me some feedback on my performance."
"I was wondering if the show has already started."
Key rule: embedded question word order
When you embed a question inside a polite phrase, the word order changes to statement order (subject before verb):
Direct question: "What time does the show start?"
Polite version: "Do you know what time the show starts?" (subject + verb, not "does the show start")
Direct question: "Where is the dressing room?"
Polite version: "Could you tell me where the dressing room is?" (not "where is the dressing room")
Adding "please" for extra politeness
You can add "please" to make any polite question even more respectful:
"Could you please help me with my lines?"
"Would you mind please lowering your voice?"
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using question word order inside a polite phrase.
Incorrect: "Do you know what does 'meander' mean?"
Correct: "Do you know what 'meander' means?"
Mistake 2: Forgetting the question mark.
Incorrect: "Could you help me with this."
Correct: "Could you help me with this?"
Practice
Rewrite each direct question as a polite question:
"What does 'sabotage' mean?" (Do you know...)
"Can you help me fix this costume?" (Would you mind...)
"Where is the nearest exit?" (Could you tell me...)
"Will you attend the rehearsal tomorrow?" (Would you be able to...)
"What time does the show start?" (Do you happen to know...)
Answers: 1. Do you know what 'sabotage' means? 2. Would you mind helping me fix this costume? 3. Could you tell me where the nearest exit is? 4. Would you be able to attend the rehearsal tomorrow? 5. Do you happen to know what time the show starts?
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply polite question structures in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Reported Speech
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Reported speech, also known as indirect speech, is used to tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. Instead of saying "He said, 'I like music,'" you say "He said that he liked music." This is important because it allows you to share information and conversations smoothly, especially in writing. In the story, you could report what characters said: "Samira told Isaac that his story mattered more than a mask."
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Reported speech, also known as indirect speech, is used to tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. Instead of saying "He said, 'I like music,'" you say "He said that he liked music." This is important because it allows you to share information and conversations smoothly, especially in writing. In the story, you could report what characters said: "Samira told Isaac that his story mattered more than a mask."
How to change statements to reported speech
Remove the quotation marks and add that after the reporting verb.
Shift the tense back one step: present simple → past simple, present continuous → past continuous, present perfect → past perfect, past simple → past perfect, will → would, can → could.
Change the pronouns: I → he/she, we → they, my → his/her, our → their.
Change time and place words: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, now → then, here → there, this → that.
Examples:
Direct: Isaac said, "The performance caused havoc."
Reported: Isaac said that the performance had caused havoc.
Direct: Samira said, "I am tired of giving in."
Reported: Samira said that she was tired of giving in.
Direct: The teacher told the students, "You will perform tomorrow."
Reported: The teacher told the students that they would perform the next day.
Reporting questions
Yes/No questions use if or whether:
Direct: "Did you save the props?"
Reported: He asked if she had saved the props.
Wh- questions keep the question word:
Direct: "Where did you hide the mask?"
Reported: He asked where she had hidden the mask.
Important: In reported questions, use statement word order (subject before verb). Do not use a question mark.
Common reporting verbs
said — neutral: "He said that knowledge was a duty."
told — always followed by a person: "She told the audience that change was possible."
explained — giving reasons: "The teacher explained that the tense showed ongoing action."
announced — public statement: "The judge announced that Musa Academy had won."
asked — for questions: "She asked if he was ready."
warned — alerting about danger: "She warned that cheating was repugnant."
Common mistakes
Incorrect: "He said that he will come." (Tense not shifted.)
Correct: "He said that he would come."
Incorrect: "She said that I was tired." (Pronoun not changed when reporting someone else's words.)
Correct: "She said that she was tired."
Practice
Change each sentence to reported speech:
Isaac said, "I have been directing this play for over a year."
Samira told him, "Do not let this cause havoc in your head."
The judge said, "This is the most original performance we have seen today."
The boy asked Isaac, "Will you perform here again?"
Answers: 1. Isaac said that he had been directing that play for over a year. 2. Samira told him not to let that cause havoc in his head. 3. The judge said that that was the most original performance they had seen that day. 4. The boy asked Isaac if/whether he would perform there again.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply reported speech in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Reflexive Pronouns
grammar.explanation:
What they are and why they matter
Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same person or thing. When someone does something to themselves, we use a reflexive pronoun. This matters because it helps us be clear in our communication. For example, "She expressed herself through the performance" shows that she did the expressing for her own benefit, not someone else.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they are and why they matter
Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same person or thing. When someone does something to themselves, we use a reflexive pronoun. This matters because it helps us be clear in our communication. For example, "She expressed herself through the performance" shows that she did the expressing for her own benefit, not someone else.
The reflexive pronouns
How to use reflexive pronouns
Place the reflexive pronoun after the verb or at the end of the sentence:
"She dressed herself in the costume."
"We enjoyed ourselves at the performance."
"He taught himself how to play the guitar."
"They prepared themselves for the big show."
Reflexive pronouns for emphasis:
"Isaac himself directed the entire play." (Emphasis: he did it personally.)
"I will do it myself." (Emphasis: without anyone's help.)
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using the wrong reflexive pronoun.
Incorrect: "She enjoyed himself at the party."
Correct: "She enjoyed herself at the party."
Mistake 2: Omitting the reflexive pronoun when needed.
Incorrect: "He taught to play the guitar."
Correct: "He taught himself to play the guitar."
Quick memory trick
Think of reflexive pronouns as a mirror. When you look in a mirror, you see yourself. In the same way, when a reflexive pronoun appears in a sentence, it reflects the action back to the subject.
Practice
Complete each sentence with the correct reflexive pronoun:
She expressed __________ through a gaudy costume.
I taught __________ how to play the oud.
They prepared __________ for the big performance.
He looked at __________ in the mirror before going on stage.
We enjoyed __________ at the drama festival.
You should believe in __________.
Answers: 1. herself  2. myself  3. themselves  4. himself  5. ourselves  6. yourself

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Role-Play Interview with a Performer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
In this activity, one person plays the role of a journalist and the other plays the role of a young performer who has just won a competition. The journalist must ask thoughtful questions about the performance, the preparation, and the emotions involved. The performer must answer in character, using vocabulary from this unit and the Present Perfect Continuous tense where appropriate.
How to prepare:
Choose a character from the story (Isaac, Samira, or another team member) or invent your own performer.
The journalist should prepare at least five questions. Use polite question structures: "Could you tell us about...?", "Would you mind explaining...?", "Do you know what inspired...?"
The performer should prepare answers that include Present Perfect Continuous: "I have been rehearsing for months", "We have been working on this play since September."
Perform the interview for your family, classmates, or AI tutor. Record it if possible.
After the interview, the journalist should write a short summary (four to five sentences) using reported speech: "Isaac explained that he had been directing his own play for over a year. He added that the missing prop had made the team feel vulnerable."
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise role-play interview with a performer and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Group Discussion — Music Across Cultures
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Discuss the following questions with a partner, a group, your family, or your AI tutor. Try to speak for at least one to two minutes for each question. Use opinion phrases such as "I believe that...", "In my view...", "It seems to me that..." and support your views with examples.
What kinds of music are popular in your community? Why do you think people enjoy them?
Can you think of a time when music or a performance brought people together? Describe what happened.
The story says that "art only matters when it is honest." Do you agree? Why or why not?
Some people say that music is more powerful than words. What do you think? Can music communicate things that language cannot?
How can the performing arts help young people develop confidence, teamwork, and creativity?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise group discussion — music across cultures and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Dramatic Reading
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the most dramatic scenes from "The Curtain Never Lies" and perform it as a dramatic reading. You can do this alone or with a partner. Focus on using your voice to convey emotion: change your tone for different characters, pause for effect at dramatic moments, and speak clearly so your audience can follow the story.
Suggested scenes:
The moment Isaac discovers the missing mask and Samira calms him down.
The climax on stage when Isaac improvises with the torn cloth.
The confrontation between Isaac and Horizon East's leader after the performance.
Isaac's conversation with the young boy in Johannesburg after the international showcase.
After your dramatic reading, ask your audience (or your AI tutor) for feedback. What was the strongest moment? Which emotions came through most clearly? What could you improve?

interview me as Isaac from "The Curtain Never Lies"? I want to
practise answering questions using the Present Perfect Continuous
and reported speech.'

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
This unit includes two writing projects. The first asks you to write a performance review of a play, concert, or artistic event. The second asks you to write a persuasive speech about the importance of the arts. Both projects will help you develop your ability to write clearly, use the grammar you have learned, and express your ideas with confidence.
Project A: Write a Performance Review
A performance review is a piece of writing that describes and evaluates a live performance such as a play, concert, dance, or poetry reading. Good reviews describe what happened, explain what was effective, and offer a balanced opinion. Your task is to write a review of a performance, either real or imagined. You may review a performance you have actually attended, or you may write a review of Musa Academy's performance from the story. The review should be between 250 and 350 words.
Performance review structure:
Introduction: State what the performance was, who performed it, where and when it took place, and give your overall impression in one sentence.
Summary of the performance: Briefly describe what happened on stage. Do not retell the entire plot; focus on the key moments.
What worked well: Describe the strongest aspects of the performance. Was the acting convincing? Was the music moving? Were the costumes and set design effective? Use specific examples.
What could be improved: Offer constructive suggestions. Was anything unclear? Did the pacing feel uneven? Were there moments that lost the audience's attention?
Conclusion: Give your final opinion and say whether you would recommend the performance to others. End with a memorable final sentence.
Before you submit, check your review against this checklist:
My review has a clear introduction, body paragraphs, and a conclusion.
I have used at least five vocabulary words from this unit.

quizzes.question: What does 'havoc' mean?
quizzes.options: A situation in which there is a lot of damage or confusion. | To arrange or use something in a certain way for a purpose. | To gather together courage, strength, or support for a specific purpose. | To make something become a certain way; to perform or deliver.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A situation in which there is a lot of damage or confusion.
quizzes.explanation: The storm caused havoc in the town, knocking down trees and power lines.

quizzes.question: What does 'deft' mean?
quizzes.options: Skilful and quick in movement or action. | The way a person walks or moves on their feet. | To arrange or use something in a certain way for a purpose. | To gather together courage, strength, or support for a specific purpose.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Skilful and quick in movement or action.
quizzes.explanation: The deft musician played the oud beautifully, impressing everyone in the audience.

quizzes.question: What does 'render' mean?
quizzes.options: A twisting or bending of the body into unusual shapes. | The way a person walks or moves on their feet. | To arrange or use something in a certain way for a purpose. | To make something become a certain way; to perform or deliver.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make something become a certain way; to perform or deliver.
quizzes.explanation: The artist will render a beautiful painting for the school art show.

quizzes.question: What does 'muster' mean?
quizzes.options: A twisting or bending of the body into unusual shapes. | The way a person walks or moves on their feet. | To gather together courage, strength, or support for a specific purpose. | To look at two or more things to see how they are similar or different.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To gather together courage, strength, or support for a specific purpose.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher asked us to muster our courage and speak in front of the class.

quizzes.question: What does 'deploy' mean?
quizzes.options: A twisting or bending of the body into unusual shapes. | Not friendly or not interested in talking to others. | To arrange or use something in a certain way for a purpose. | To look at two or more things to see how they are similar or different.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To arrange or use something in a certain way for a purpose.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher decided to deploy new teaching methods to help students learn better.
